item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think this is a "Safe" SCP by current standards. Arguably it's Keter, because, as the author says above,

Rabbits can migrate, you miss one or two and they breed like, well, rabbits, so in a few years you've got the same problem again; dealing with this requires a large scale effort (you've got to comb every inch of half of Georgia), and the Foundation finds it easier to amnesticize ~20 people per year affected by this.

If the Foundation can't feasibly contain an anomaly, and constant vigilance and regular follow-up action is required in order to address instances' effects after-the-fact, that's Keter, right? I'm still learning, so I'm open to hearing explanations to the contrary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked the overall feel of this one. It's really unique in how it transmits a sensation of loneliness comparable to seeing someone fall in love with a fictional character: someone's stuck in their own mind living their own invisible fictions. I didn't understand what the last bit with the carrots is about, but the rest is well executed.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very cute in the first half, and slightly dark at the end. This skip is a good reminder that a skip doesn't have to be hyper-unique, a format-screw, based in horror, or reason #23469 the world might end tomorrow in order to be a good skip. I hope more people find this one and give it a vote.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oryctolagus species are native to Europe. This takes place in the Chattanooga valley, so you want a Sylvilagus species. You'll also have to fix the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but for god's sake put some tags.

The article has only been up for about a day or so, and the team that handles tagging articles is neither paid nor possessed with unnatural tagging speed. Tagging will happen eventually. There's no need to sound so imperative about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You realize that’s not the author’s job, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An entertaining idea, but for god's sake put some tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how the scip alters in a surprising shift of tone in a way that is well-done and doesn't feel forced nor awkward. The overall premise of the scip interests me, and I give you kudos for that.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good answer - there's certainly no reason to change what the skip is about if you already have a plan for what it's about, and that is a strong theme to explore. I'm not sure that it really gets there (and I wonder whether showing the loss of fictions without the amnestics angle might help), but I respect the intention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm, good point. I should say any unmarried "adult."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see where you are coming from. But this skip is ultimately about (ordinary human) relationships — how they are inevitably based on the construction of fictions we tell ourselves about our partner; how we need someone not just to love us, but also occasionally disapprove of us; and how, despite the artifice of it all, the loss of the fictions we tell ourselves can be tragic. Because this is the main point here, I didn't really want to explore the various permutations of what can happen with this anomaly.

I have answers in my head about attempting to contain the rabbit population [1], but I'm not sure if they should be edited in, for the same reason as above — this skip is meant to focus on something else. I guess it depends on how big of an omission this is; let's see if other readers have the same reaction.

[1] Rabbits can migrate, you miss one or two and they breed like, well, rabbits, so in a few years you've got the same problem again; dealing with this requires a large scale effort (you've got to comb every inch of half of Georgia), and the Foundation finds it easier to amnesticize ~20 people per year affected by this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love SCPs that start out kind of humorous but shift to darkness by the end. Nicely done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels like there are so many angles you could take this idea (eg what happens if a subject sees its rabbit breeding? does each affected subject pick a different rabbit and if not, then what? what if their pet dog kills the rabbit? what if the happy couple leaves Chattanooga valley? what if the rabbit has baby rabbits? not all marriages are happy - how does that work with rabbits?) and the choice to go with "it's deeper than amnestics" seems like a really unproductive one. It doesn't really lead anywhere, and I think that is evidenced by the third log which takes a very long time to make a carrot joke.

There's also the implication that amnestics don't work effectively when it comes to the wiping of any long-term partner, which slightly undermines how they're treated elsewhere. And why do the Containment Procedures make no attempt to (a) contain the rabbit population; or (b) restrict access / habitation in the area of effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a simple man. I see my home state, I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how abruptly this shifts from whimsical to upsetting. Kudos on keeping everything so concise, too. The carrot monitoring in the containment procedures was a really nice touch. Lots to like here, really. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quick question, you say that:

Any unmarried person residing in the valley for over six months will begin developing romantic feelings towards a specimen of SCP-3180

Would this include children? If a divorced parent moved with there kid(s) would they all be infected?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image is in the public domain: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_rabbit#/media/File:Oryctolagus_cuniculus_Tasmania_2.jpg

Thanks to Leveritas for giving me crit in the draft forum.
